evals.prompt: Clean up this module, it's a mess.
evals.expected_output: Activates quality: runs a behavior-preserving refactor-in-place — upgrades the unit's quality tier (rename for intent, collapse indirection, clarify control flow, tighten naming) rather than sprawling new code, cites bus-first (reuse before rewrite, smallest change), keeps the existing tests green before and after, and never removes load-bearing guards (validation/security/error-handling/accessibility).

evals.prompt: This logic is duplicated in three places — consolidate it.
evals.expected_output: Activates quality: the recycler move — salvages the duplication back onto the shared bus lane and routes the callers through it (citing bus-first: tap the existing line instead of keeping parallel copies), deletes the now-dead copies, and confirms identical behavior with the existing tests staying green.

evals.prompt: refacter this messy file for clearity, dont change behaviour
evals.expected_output: Despite the typos, activates quality: a behavior-preserving in-place refactor for clarity (upgrade the unit's tier, recycle dead/duplicated code), citing bus-first, with the existing tests green before and after and load-bearing guards untouched.

evals.prompt: This endpoint is slow — make it faster.
evals.expected_output: Does NOT activate quality; routes to modules for performance/efficiency (find the bottleneck, measure, optimize where returns are real). quality is behavior-preserving and never trades correctness or a guard for speed.

evals.prompt: Add a new export endpoint to the API.
evals.expected_output: Does NOT activate quality; routes to roboports to implement the new tracked behavior end-to-end. quality only improves existing code in place without changing behavior, so adding a new endpoint is out of scope.
